coredata.dc:title: Excellent dispersion effects of carbon nanodots on the thermoelectric properties of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 with excessive Te
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xin
Cao, Ruijuan
Zhang, Yuewen
Tian, Zengguo
Li, Xin-Jian
Song, Hongzhang
coredata.dc:description: With excellent performance at room temperature as well as being the most mature n-type thermoelectric material in commercialization, Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 has been attracting increased attention. In this study, its carrier concentration and mobility were optimized by introducing excessive Te. When the excessive content of Te was 0.4, its power factor was the largest, and its thermal conductivity declined slightly. Based on Te excess, carbon nanodots were introduced as the dispersed nanosecond phase. The power factors were improved further due to the energy filter effect. In particular, the lattice thermal conductivity was depressed remarkably due to enhanced phonon scattering. Finally, at 423 K, the maximum ZT value of the sample reached 0.91, which was approximately 42% higher than that of the pure Bi2Te2.7Se0.3. The experimental results demonstrate that carbon nanodots dispersion acting as a nanosecond phase is an effective method to improve the thermoelectric performance of n-type Bi2Te2.7Se0.3-based materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi2Te2.7Se0.3
Thermoelectric properties
Te excess
Carbon nanodots dispersion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant no. 11974016 ) and the Henan Educational Committee Foundation (Grant no. 20A140028 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 (a) powders and (b) bulks.
SEM images of Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 powders (a) g = 0 and (b) g = 0.4.
SEM images of Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 bulks (a) g = 0(b) g = 0.2 (c) g = 0.4 (d) g = 0.6 (e) g = 0.8 (f) g = 0.4.
Temperature (or content)-dependent (a) resistivity, (b) Hall carrier concentration and mobility, (c) Seebeck coefficients, (d) power factors.
(a)Thermal conductivity, (b) lattice thermal conductivity of Bi2Te2.7+gSe0.3.
SEM images of hot-pressed Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs samples (a) f = 0, (b) f = 0.05, (c) f = 0.1, (d) f = 0.15, (e) f = 0.2.
BSE of hot-pressed bulk samples with 0.15 wt% CDs.

Temperature-dependent thermoelectric performance of Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs samples, (a) resistivity, (b) Seebeck coefficients, (c) power factors, (d) thermal conductivity, (e) lattice thermal conductivity, (f) ZT.

head.ce:title: Excellent dispersion effects of carbon nanodots on the thermoelectric properties of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 with excessive Te

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Material Physics of Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Material Physics of Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Material Physics of Ministry of Education, School of Physics and Microelectronics, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With excellent performance at room temperature as well as being the most mature n-type thermoelectric material in commercialization, Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 has been attracting increased attention. In this study, its carrier concentration and mobility were optimized by introducing excessive Te. When the excessive content of Te was 0.4, its power factor was the largest, and its thermal conductivity declined slightly. Based on Te excess, carbon nanodots were introduced as the dispersed nanosecond phase. The power factors were improved further due to the energy filter effect. In particular, the lattice thermal conductivity was depressed remarkably due to enhanced phonon scattering. Finally, at 423 K, the maximum ZT value of the sample reached 0.91, which was approximately 42% higher than that of the pure Bi2Te2.7Se0.3. The experimental results demonstrate that carbon nanodots dispersion acting as a nanosecond phase is an effective method to improve the thermoelectric performance of n-type Bi2Te2.7Se0.3-based materials.

ce:section.ce:para: In the twenty-first century, aggravated energy crises have made the development of energy materials increasingly attractive [1–3]. Thermoelectric materials, being new energy materials, provide a new idea for solving a series of ecological problems, such as climate change, water resources, and air pollution [4]. With the continuous progress of research, the system of thermoelectric materials is becoming increasingly larger, such as SiGe [5,6], Cu2Se [7,8], SnSe [9,10], oxide thermoelectric materials [11–13] in the high-temperature region (800–1000 K), Pb-Te alloys [14,15], Mg-Si alloys [16,17] in the middle-temperature region (400–700 K), and Bi2Te3 [18,19] at low temperature (near room temperature (RT)). Bi2Te3-based alloys are the most advanced RT thermoelectric material in the past few decades, and their performance is constantly improving [20,21]. They have wide application value in the business field and crucial research significance in the scientific research field.

In recent years, the maturity of nanotechnology has significantly improved the thermoelectric properties of Bi2Te3. The dimensionless figure of merit ZT determines the thermoelectric performance of the material and is expressed as [22].
(1)
ZT =
S
2
σ
κ
T
Where S is the Seebeck coefficient, ρ is the resistivity, S and ρ together characterize the electrical transport properties of the material. κ is the thermal conductivity, which characterizes the thermal transport properties of the material. T is the absolute temperature at the time of testing. In 2008, Poudel et al. [23] successfully prepared a p-type BiSbTe bulk alloy using nanostructured method. From room temperature to 373 K, the ZT value increased from 1.2 to 1.4. In 2010, Shen et al. [24] used a hot pressing method to prepare Bi0.5Sb1.5Te3 alloy, and used the hot forging process to adjust the alloy’s microstructure to achieve improved thermoelectric properties. The ZT peak value of the hot-forged sample is greater than 1.3 at 300 K, which is 1.5 times that of unhot-forged samples. In 2014, Hu et al. [25] made the ZT value of n-type polycrystalline Bi2Te2.3Se0.7 reach 1.2 at 445 K and p-type polycrystalline Bi0.3Sb1.7Te3 reach 1.3 at 380 K by combining ball milling and thermal deformation methods. In 2018, Li et al. [26] introduced InSb nanoparticles into BiTeSe and obtained high thermoelectric properties at 323 K (ZT = 1.2) by maintaining the original high PF and reducing the lattice thermal conductivity. In 2020, Zhu et al. [27] proposed that excess Te was conducive to improving thermoelectric properties of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3. Then the element Sb was used to replace the Bi site. Finally, the ZT value increased to 1.4. In 2021, Li et al. [28] achieved the maximum ZT value of 1.01 for Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 thermoelectric materials by accurately controlling the structure and composition of grain boundaries. It was 40% higher than that of commercial Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 substrates. The p-type Bi2Te3 system has developed rapidly, but the development of the matching n-type Bi2Te3 material is relatively backward, which is not conducive to the wide application of Bi2Te3 thermoelectric materials near room temperature. Therefore, this study will primarily discuss the appropriate means that may improve the ZT value of n-type Bi2Te2.7Se0.3.

Huong et al. [29] reported that p-type Bi1.8Sb0.2Te3+δ alloys added with excessive Te became n-type materials. In the p-type (Bi, Sb)2Te3 alloy, adding excessive Te can inhibit the generation of anti-defects and reduce the carrier concentration. When excessive Te exceeded 0.276, it could even form the anti-defect of Te replacing Bi, showing n-type conductivity. Osamu Yamashita et al. [30] prepared n-type Bi2Te2.72Se0.18 with excessive Te, but they did not systematically study the change mechanism of thermoelectric properties. In 2015, Kim et al. [31] formed lattice distortion at the low-energy grain boundary by squeezing liquid Te, which effectively scattered the intermediate frequency phonons and considerably reduced lattice thermal conductivity. Finally, the ZT value of p-type Bi0.5Sb1.5Te3 alloy is up to 1.86 at 320 K. In 2021, Sun et al. [32] prepared Bi2Te2.5Se0.5 + 0.3 wt% Te sample using melting and high-energy ball milling method. The ZT value of Bi2Te3 is significantly increased under the double influence of excessive Te and Se doping, and finally reached 1.0 at 398 K. Excessive Te can effectively increase the ZT value of Bi2Te3-based alloys, but there is scant literature on the effects of different preparation methods on their properties. This study intends to use the hydrothermal method and the followed hot-pressing method to prepare n-type Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 materials and explore the content of excessive Te to obtain the optimal thermoelectric properties.

Nunna et al. [33] prepared Cu2Se/CNTs hybrid materials. CNTs and Cu2Se matrix were well combined and uniformly dispersed. Finally, the thermoelectric merit value reached 2.4 at 1000 K. Wang et al. [34] introduced nano-SiC into SnTe, which significantly increased the ZT value by increasing the carrier concentration and reducing the lattice thermal conductivity. Eventually, the SnTe + 1 vol% SiC materials obtained the maximum ZT value of approximately 0.7. Li et al. [35] studied the thermoelectric properties of GNs/Bi0.4Sb1.6Te3 materials. The addition of graphene simultaneously optimized the electrical conductivity and thermal conductivity, making its ZT value reach 1.54 at 440 K. Our previous studies also proved that the nano-composite method can reduce the material’s thermal conductivity effectively [36–38]. As a new zero-dimensional carbon-based material, carbon nanodots (CDs) have a series of advantages such as environmental friendliness, wide sources of raw materials, and low cost. These are widely used in scientific research [39–42]. Compared with one-dimensional CNTs and two-dimensional graphene, the size of CDs is smaller, which should be conducive to reducing the thermal conductivity. The combination of Te excess and CDs dispersion can simultaneously regulate the thermoelectric properties of n-type Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 from multiple aspects, and the effect is more evident.

In this experiment, two sets of sample powders were prepared using the hydrothermal method, and then the powders were formed into blocks after hot pressing. The chemical raw materials used in the experiment are BiCl3(AR, SCR), tellurium powder (5N, SCR), selenium powder (AR, Kermel), NaBH4(98%, SCR), EDTA(AR, Kermel), NaOH(AR, Kermel), CH3COCH3(AR, HengXing), CH3CH2OH(AR, HengXing), and CDs prepared by Shen et al. [43].

The reaction raw materials BiCl3, tellurium powder, and selenium powder were weighed according to the stoichiometric ratio Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 (g = 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8) and then placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) lining. Quantitative surfactant EDTA, alkaline agent NaOH, and 200 ml deionized water were added in turn. After 30 min of stirring with a magnetic stirrer, 0.35 g of NaBH4 was added as a reducing agent. The solution was stirred again for 5 min. The PTFE lining was sealed and put into a stainless steel shell. Finally, the reaction kettle was tightened and placed in a drying oven for heating reaction. After the reaction was completed, the reaction kettle was removed at room temperature. To remove impurities such as organic matter in the powder, the suspension was washed with deionized water, alcohol, and acetone for several times. The powder obtained after extraction and filtration needed to be dried at 333 K for 10 h.

CDs were dissolved in deionized water at a certain concentration. The solution was stirred for more than 12 h, and an aqueous solution of CDs was prepared for later use. The preparation method of the powder of Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs (f = 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15 and 0.2) was the same as the previous one, but the corresponding amount of CDs aqueous solution is to be added and the solution stirred for 5 min before sealing the reactor. The rest of the experiment process was the same.

The powder prepared by the hydrothermal method was hot pressed in a vacuum hot-pressing furnace. The specific process is as follows: First, the contact surface between the mold and the sample was sprayed with boron nitride release agent, and then the powder was placed in the graphite mold. Second, the mold was placed in a vacuum hot-pressing furnace. When the vacuum degree in the furnace cavity was lower than 1 Pa, the hot-pressing furnace began to heat up at a rate of 10 K/min. Third, when the set temperature (500 ℃) was reached, a pressure of 60 MPa was applied to the mold. The pressure was relieved after 15 min. Fourth, the instrument was cooled to about 300 K, and the bulk can be taken out of the mold.

X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical, Netherlands) with Cu Kα radiation was used to measure the phase structure of sample powder and bulk. XRD data can be analyzed using MDI-Jade 6.5, which is beneficial to determine the composition of samples and impurity peaks. The sample’s microscopic morphology and backscatter analysis were observed with a field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM).

Various tests were conducted on the hot-pressed bulk. The LSR system (LSR-3/800 Seebeck coefficients/ electrical resistance measuring system Linseis, Germany) was used to measure electrical performance. The Seebeck coefficient and resistivity of the sample can be obtained by using helium as a protective gas in the test. The thermal conductivity characterized the material’s heat transport properties and can be calculated using the following formula:
(2)
κ = D
C
p
ρ
where D is the thermal diffusion coefficient of the sample, which was measured by LFA457 system (Netzsch, Germany);
C
p
is the specific heat capacity of the sample, which was measured by a DSC differential scanning calorimeter; and ρ is the density of the sample, which was calculated by Archimedes principle. To test the carrier concentration and mobility, the electric transport property test system produced by Beijing East Changing Technologies was used.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns for Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 (g = 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8) nanoparticle powders and bulks. All diffraction peaks correspond to the standard card of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 (PDF#050–0954). In Fig. 1(a), the addition of excessive Te makes the powder’s XRD pattern appear to be a Te diffraction peak and the peak intensity gradually increases, indicating that excessive Te exists in its own atomic form. However, in the bulk’s XRD pattern (Fig. 1b), the diffraction peak of Te is not found, indicating that the excessive Te in the powder disappears after the hot-pressing process.

In Fig. 2, the powders of Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 series samples have a hexagonal flake morphology approximately 100–500 nm long and less than 50 nm thick. Compared with Bi2Te2.7Se0.3, the size and morphology of Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 powder do not change, indicating that excessive Te does not influence the structure of the Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 nanopowder. In our previous research, the bulk sample prepared from flower powder has lower electrical resistivity and thermal conductivity, and finally obtains the highest ZT value [44,45].

For Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 series of samples, the densities of each sample with g = 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8 are 7.39, 7.41, 7.30, 7.33, and 7.27 g/cm3, respectively. The density of all samples exceeds 90% of the theoretical density. The cross-sectional SEM picture of the hot-pressed sample also shows that the sample is relatively dense. The crystal grain of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 sample maintains the layered morphology of powder without obvious directional distribution, and the grain size is approximately 2
μ m
. The grain size of the sample with g = 0.2 ( Fig. 3b) increases significantly. The maximum size reaches 10
μ m
and the average size reaches approximately 5
μ m
. As the amount of Te increased, the grains become larger. However, the grain sizes of the three samples with g = 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8 are not significantly different, and small grains appear around the large, layered grain boundaries. Fig. 3f is a magnified SEM of the sample with g = 0.4. The small grains have a hexagonal structure and exist at the boundary or middle of the larger grain boundaries. Due to the sample fractured naturally, voids appear in the middle of the grain, which is caused by the shedding of small particles during the fracture process.

During the hot-pressing process, Te becomes a liquid phase and wraps around the crystal grains. After pressure is applied, the flow of the liquid phase will promote mirror slippage, so that the crystal grains will extend along the a-b plane, and the size will become larger, achieving the effect of liquid-phase sintering [46–48]. The energy of the grain boundary formed by liquid-phase sintering is low, which has little effect on carrier scattering. However, low-energy grain boundaries are prone to form periodic dislocations, which enhance the scattering of intermediate frequency phonons in the form of point defects and boundary scattering [31]. Studies [49,50] confirm that in p-type materials, Sb-rich phases appear at the grain boundaries when excessive Sb is added, and Te-rich phases are observed when excessive Te is added in n-type materials. Although excessive Te is extruded during hot pressing, 6% of Te remains in the sample [51]. When there is excessive Te, small particles will be precipitated around the grain boundaries. These changes in microscopic morphology will affect carrier transport and phonon transport, which is beneficial to improve the ZT value of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3.

Fig. 4 clearly shows the change trend of the electrical properties of Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 samples with temperature. As temperature increases, the sample’s resistivity also increases, which is a significant feature of metal semiconductors. The content of excessive Te in the sample keeps increasing, and the resistivity shows a trend of first decreasing and then increasing. When g = 0.4, the resistivity reaches the minimum value, which is only 5.35
μ Ω . m
at room temperature. According to the formula of resistivity,
(3)
ρ =
1
μ ne

The sample’s resistivity is related to the change of carrier concentration and mobility. The change trends of n and μ are shown in Fig. 4b. In n-type Bi2Te3, the evaporation energy of Te (52.6 KJ/mol) is less than that of Bi (104.9 KJ/mol) [52]. Compared with Bi, Te is easier to volatilize to form
V
Te
2 +
vacancies and contribute two electrons [53]. The specific formation process is as follows:
(4)
Bi
2
Te
3
→ 2
Bi
Bi
×
+
3 − x
Te
Te
×
+ xTe
g
↑ + x
V
Te
2 +
+ 2 x
e
−

In addition, due to the lack of Te, the dangling bonds at the grain boundaries can also be regarded as Te vacancies [50]. It is worth noting that each Te vacancy can provide two electrons, which greatly increases the carrier concentration. Therefore, the carrier concentration shows an increasing trend after the sample is added with excessive Te, which has been confirmed by experimental results. The carrier mobility increases first and then decreases with the increase of g and reaches the maximum when g = 0.4. This change trend is related to the change of the sample microstructure. The sample’s grain size gradually increases, the grain boundary decreases, and the carrier scattering decreases, which facilitate the carrier’s migration on the a-b plane, and the mobility increases continuously. Compared with the three samples of g = 0.4, 0.6, and 0.8, with the increase of g, the crystal grains do not continue to grow. However, small-sized granular crystal grains began to appear around the grain boundaries, which can increase the grain boundaries and enhance the carrier scattering. Therefore, the mobility starts to decline again. In general, under the double influence of carrier concentration and mobility, a lower resistivity can be obtained by adding an appropriate amount of excessive Te.

In the test temperature range, the Seebeck coefficients of Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 series samples are all negative, which is a typical n-type semiconductor. The Seebeck coefficient of samples with normal stoichiometric ratio is significantly higher than that of samples with excessive Te. As the Te content increases, the variation tendency of Seebeck coefficient initially decreases and then increases. When g = 0.4, the Seebeck coefficient reaches the minimum. For metal materials such as Bi-Te alloy, the Seebeck coefficient is related to the following physical quantities [54]:
(5)
S =
8
π
2
k
B
2
/ 3 e
h
2
m
*
T
π / 3 n
2 / 3
where
k
B
, h ,
m
*
, T , and n represent Boltzmann constant, Planck constant, effective electron mass, absolute temperature, and carrier concentration, respectively. According to formula (5), the increase of carrier concentration is the important reason that the Seebeck coefficients of the Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 samples are lower than the Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 samples. As the temperature continues to rise, the Seebeck coefficient of all samples began to decrease. This is because a large number of electron-hole pairs are generated by intrinsic excitation in the high-temperature range [35,55], which leads to an increase in minority carriers and the decrease of Seebeck coefficient [56]. Based on the intrinsic excitation and the bipolar diffusion effect, the Seebeck coefficient can be expressed by the dual-band model as
(6)
S =
S
n
σ
n
+
S
p
σ
p
σ
n
+
σ
p
where n and p represent the electron and hole components, respectively. Because p-type components and n-type components have completely opposite Seebeck coefficients, the material’s Seebeck coefficient will decrease significantly. Studies [55,57] propose that higher carrier concentration can inhibit or delay the occurrence of intrinsic excitation and bipolar effect. In this set of experiments, the Te excess sample has higher carrier concentration; thus, the thermal excitation temperature of the samples with excessive Te is higher than that of the Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 samples, and the deterioration of the Seebeck coefficient is slower. Near 550 K, the Seebeck coefficient of each sample reaches a similar value. A similar phenomenon occurs in the Bi2Te3 + x wt% Te system [32].

The sample’s Seebeck coefficient and resistivity are important parameters to measure the electrical transport performance of thermoelectric materials. In general, a decrease in resistivity is accompanied by a decrease in Seebeck coefficient, which has diametrically opposite effects on electrical transport performance [58]. PF can directly reflect the material’s electrical transport performance, and its variation trend is bounded by g = 0.4, which is similar to the variation rule of resistivity and Seebeck coefficient. When g < 0.4, PF shows an increasing trend, and when g > 0.4, PF shows a decreasing trend. The electrical performance of the Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 sample is the best, and its PF is approximately 2156
μ W / m .
K
2
at 350 K. In this experiment, the decrease of resistivity seems to play a dominant role, so the electrical transport performance of Te excess sample is significantly improved compared with that of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 sample.

Fig. 5 shows the relationship between the thermal performance of the Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 series of samples and temperature. Compared with Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 samples, the thermal conductivity of Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 has different degrees of reduction. This phenomenon can be explained from the perspective of microscopic morphology. A small number of small crystal grains with a diameter of approximately 100 nm can be found in the amplified SEM of Bi2Te2.7 + 0.4Se0.3 (Fig. 4f). These small hexagonal grains exist at the boundary and middle of large grains with a size of about 10 µm, and a hole appears in the middle. This type of microstructure enhances phonon scattering ability and reduces the lattice thermal conductivity, thus significantly reducing the total thermal conductivity. Another factor that causes the thermal conductivity to decrease is that excessive Te forms a liquid phase during the sintering process, which promotes crystal plane, and, as a result, dislocations appear at the grain boundaries, which effectively scatter phonons. The next result is that the lattice thermal conductivity decreases, which is consistent with the experimental results of Kim et al. [31]. They use a simple liquid-phase compression process in which there is an instantaneous flow of the liquid phase during the pressing process. This process greatly reduces the
κ
ι
(320 Κ , 0.33
W
m
− 1
K
− 1
). In the experiment of preparing Bi0.4Sb1.6Te3+x samples by hot pressing, Sb-rich phases exist between the crystal lattices. Kim posits that the phonon scattering of Sb between the crystal lattices reduces the material’s lattice thermal conductivity. In general, whether it is the formation of lattice defects due to the slippage of crystal planes, the precipitation of fine crystal grains in situ, or the presence of Te between the crystal lattices, the phonon’s scattering can be enhanced, and the thermal conductivity becomes smaller.

Because of the bipolar effect occurring at high temperatures, the ZT of Bi2Te2.7+g Se0.3 samples increases first and then decreases, with the increasing of temperature [59]. The ZT value of the sample with g = 0.4 achieves 0.78 at 450 K, which is remarkably higher than Bi2Te2.7Se0.3. This group of experiments proves that adding excessive Te can be used as a key method of promoting the thermoelectric performance of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3. Fig. 6.

The theoretical density of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 is 7.78
g /
cm
3
. For the samples with f = 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, and 0.2, the densities are 7.28, 7.39, 7.42, 7.45, and 7.26
g /
cm
3
, respectively, reaching more than 90% of the theoretical density. As shown in Fig. 7, the Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs sample is dense. The hot-pressed sample still maintains an obvious layered structure, with a grain size of approximately 10 µm and a thickness of approximately 100 nm. After introducing excessive Te, the phenomenon of liquid-phase sintering occurs, resulting in the appearance of large grains. There are small hexagonal nanoparticles at grain boundaries, and some of them are wrapped in the large particles. When the sample ruptures naturally, they fall off from the large particles, leaving holes. In the previous experiment, it has been confirmed that materials with such micromorphology have higher thermoelectric properties.

Comparing the samples with different CDs dispersion, it was found that the microstructure does not change significantly. In other words, CDs as the second nanophase do not affect the growth of the substrate grains. This is inconsistent with the result of Bi2Te3 compounding with CNTs studied by Cao et al. [36]. As a one-dimensional carbon material, CNTs have a large difference in morphology from the substrate, which can be observed from SEM. CDs are zero-dimensional carbon materials with small size, and their morphology is not significantly different from that of the substrate, so they cannot be identified in the cross-sectional SEM. In addition, because the dispersion of CDs is too small, XRD cannot detect the diffraction peaks of CDs. The XRD patterns of all samples in this group are the same as Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 in Fig. 1, so they will not be shown specifically.

In order to study the distribution of CDs in the sample, backscattering is observed on the sample with 0.15 wt% CDs dispersion. The results are shown in Fig. 8. CDs are uniformly distributed in the sample substrate and the size is approximately several hundred nanometers. Compared with the micron-level crystal grains of the substrate sample, the size difference between CDs and grains is approximately 20-fold, which is consistent with the design requirements of the micron-level crystal grains and nanosecond phase composite materials. This composite material is proven to be prone to grain boundary scattering, drastically reducing the material’s lattice thermal conductivity. At the same time, a potential barrier can be formed at the interface to form an energy filtering effect and improve the material’s Seebeck coefficients.

Fig. 9 shows the thermoelectric properties of the Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs sample. Within the tested temperature range, the bulk sample’s resistivity is proportional to the temperature, which is a characteristic of metal semiconductors. As the amount of CDs increases, the sample’s resistivity continues to rise, exhibiting barely any change in the resistivity of the sample with the minimum dispersion. The variation in resistivity can be explained in several aspects. The carrier concentration of n-type materials is mainly affected by VTe vacancies. For polycrystalline samples, the Te dangling bonds at the grain boundaries are the same as VTe vacancies, which suppress the donor doping effect and reduce the carrier concentration. After the introduction of the nanosecond phase, the grain boundaries increase. While phonons are scattered, carriers are also scattered; therefore, the carrier mobility is reduced. In other words, the resistivity of the n-type material combined with the nanosecond phase will increase. When Cao et al. introduced CNTs into Lu0.1Bi1.9Te3, the change tendency of resistivity initially decreased and then increased [36]. This is because C-based nanomaterials are conductors with good performance, and the addition of a small amount can provide a specific conductive path [60–62], which reduces the composite material’s resistivity. A large number of C-based nanomaterials will be scattered around the grain boundary, and there is a barrier with the substrate grain boundary, which plays the role of energy filtering. CDs are zero-dimensional carbon materials, and a small amount of compounding will have a similar effect. However, unlike one-dimensional CNTs or two-dimensional graphene, the effect is not evident. Therefore, when a small amount CDs is added, the resistivity does not change significantly. However, when the amount of addition is large, a large number of grain boundaries can be formed, hindering the carrier’s movement and increasing the resistivity.

In Fig. 9(b), the sample’s Seebeck coefficients are negative, indicating that the Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs sample is an n-type semiconductor. Furthermore, the Seebeck coefficients of CDs-dispersed samples are better than that of Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 sample. The increase in Seebeck coefficient is the result of the energy filtering effect. The nanosecond phase introduces a potential barrier in the valence band or conduction band to filter out low-energy carriers and selectively pass high-energy carriers, improving the Seebeck coefficient. Although the Seebeck coefficient of the sample with the largest CDs dispersion (f=0.20) is higher than that of the sample without CDs dispersion, it is not the highest, indicating that the Seebeck coefficient does not increase monotonously with f. The excessive introduction of second phase will deteriorate the sample’s thermoelectric performance.

Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 composited with CDs can increase the Seebeck coefficient through the energy filtering effect. However, it also increases the resistivity to a certain extent due to the reduction of the carriers involved in the conduction. It makes the improvement of Seebeck coefficient to be partially offset. The power factor PF represents the overall effect of electrical transport performance and is shown in Fig. 9(c). Compared with the Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 sample, the power factor of three samples with CDs dispersion of 0.05 wt%,0.10 wt%, and 0.15 wt% are all increased to different degrees. The maximum power factor of the sample with f = 0.1 is 2568
μ W / m
K
2
.

As the CDs’ content increased, the sample’s thermal conductivity decreases. The lowest thermal conductivity is 1.01 for the sample with CDs dispersion of 0.2 at 400 K. There are several mechanisms for reducing the lattice thermal conductivity [63]. The point defects introduced element doping scatter long-wave phonons. Nanocomposite introduced nano-level microstructures, which can effectively scatter mid-wave phonons and form full frequency scattering. The grain boundaries introduced by nano-processing scatter short-wave phonons. In the Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs series of samples, Se atoms replace Te to form a solid solution and introduce alloy scattering. Nano-powders are prepared using hydrothermal method, and the bulk is sintered using hot pressing. The grains are refined, and the micron-scale grain boundary scattering is increased. The nanoscale scattering center is introduced in nanocomposite. Under multiple effects, the thermal conductivity of the Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs sample decreases obviously.

The ZT value of hot-pressed Bi2Te2.7+0.4Se0.3 + f wt% CDs sample is calculated by formula (1), and it is shown in Fig. 9(e). Except for the sample f = 0.2, the ZT values of the other CDs composite samples have been improved. The samples with CDs dispersion of 0.1 and 0.15 achieve a similar effect, and the ZT value reaches 0.91 at 423 K, which is approximately 42% higher than the maximum ZT value of the pure Bi2Te2.7Se0.3. Especially, their averaged ZT value at 320–520 K reaches ~0.81. The combination of excessive Te and CDs dispersion can effectively increase the ZT value of n-type Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 materials.

The study on the Bi2Te3-based alloys has always been a hot spot in the field of thermoelectricity. Researchers try different methods to enhance its thermoelectric performance. Hwang et al. [64] prepared Cu0.01Bi1.925Hf0.075Te2.7Se0.3 samples through conventional melt-solidification process and spark plasma sintering technology. A ZT value of 0.83 was obtained at 320 K. Zhu et al. [65] mixed the nano-Ru powder and Bi2Te3 powder uniformly by manual grinding, and finally sintered into a block by plasma electric spark. The Bi2Te3 + 1 wt% Ru sample obtained a ZT value of 0.93 at 423 K. Guo et al. [66] cold-pressed the high-energy ball-milled powder into a cylinder, and then sintering in situ. Finally, they achieve an optimal ZT of 0.94 in 505 K of n-type Bi0.92Nb0.08Te3. Zhou et al. [67] melted the high-purity raw materials sealed in quartz tubes for 10 h. The obtained ingot was ground into fine powder and then thermal deformation was processed by spark plasma sintering. The final n-type Bi1.5Sb0.5Te3 material obtained a peak ZT value of 1.0 at 330 K. Pan et al. [68] revealed the importance of combining texturing and nanostructuring techniques, and developed a maximum dimensionless figure of merit (ZT max) exceeding 1.1 at 473 K. Li et al. [69] obtained a peak zT of ∼1.1 at 625 K for the Bi1.85In0.15Te2Se + 0.25 wt% SbI3 alloy after hot deformation. Compared with other experimental methods, the hydrothermal method and hot pressing method used in this paper have the advantages of simple equipment and low price. Finally, the ZT value which is comparable to those of the above experiments is obtained.

Bi-Te alloy, as the thermoelectric material with the best performance near room temperature, is widely used commercially. However, it should be noted that thermoelectric devices concurrently require n- and p-type materials. The ZT value of the p-type material has reached 1.4, whereas the ZT value of the n-type material is lower. Therefore, successfully preparing n-type Bi2Te3-based alloys with high thermoelectric performance is the focus of scientific research at this stage. As a typical n-type material, the Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 powder in the experiment conducted is prepared using the hydrothermal method, and the bulk is prepared using compression by hot pressing. First, the thermoelectric performance of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 with excessive Te is explored. When the excess value of Te is 0.4, the electrical performance is improved, and the thermal conductivity is also reduced. The ZT value of 0.78 is obtained at 450 K. Based on the optimal Te excess, CDs is introduced as the second nanophase to further reduce the material’s thermal conductivity. When the content of introduced CDs is less than 0.2 wt%, the sample’s ZT has been significantly improved. At 423 K, the highest ZT value of 0.91 is achieved for the samples with CDs dispersion of 0.1 or 0.15, which is approximately 16.7% higher than that of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 with excessive Te and 42% higher than that of the pure Bi2Te2.7Se0.3. This study confirms that the thermoelectric performance of Bi2Te2.7Se0.3 could be significantly improved after adding excessive Te and introducing CDs.

Xin Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Ruijuan Cao: Formal analysis, Validation, Conceptualization. Yuewen Zhang: Resources. Zengguo Tian: Supervision. Xin-Jian Li: Resources, Supervision. Hongzhang Song: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
